5 min read
“I think this is a life truism…be aware when you hear a voice in your
head that says — and you’ll usually squint your eyes or you’ll hear
someone say the following words: ‘It doesn’t make sense,’ and it’s
always a sign of something really powerful.” — Adam Robinson, 2016
Interview with Tim Ferriss
When you understand how the world really works, what your
audience really wants, and how prospects and customers really
behave, most email marketing strategies and tactics don’t make
sense.
Broadly speaking, those strategies and tactics are based on the
�awed assumption that we have to act fast or we’ll lose the
opportunity to make a sale forever.
As a result, more often than not, when we type in our email
address and click “Submit” we quickly start to feel like we’ve made
a terrible mistake…
Strategic Upstream Decisions:
Emphasize Relationships
Before Transactions
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 7 --

Today…and today only…we have a deal for you…but you’d better act
fast…discount expires at midnight…stock is limited…we’re never going
to o�er this again at this price…taking this o� the market forever…
Gross.
No one likes the experience of being sold to, yet — as the saying
goes — people love to buy. Knowing that, why would we spend
our time, energy, and attention trying to force the sale when, if we
treat our audience well from the start, they’re far more likely to
buy when they’re ready?
Worse, focusing immediately on selling ignores the fact that most
people who will become customers eventually aren’t going to buy
immediately.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 7 --

Legendary marketer Dean Jackson has analyzed his clients’ data
and discovered that 85% of leads who eventually purchase do so
after 90 days.
Imagine you’ve created a course or a product, generated leads, and
you then made �fteen sales in the �rst few weeks. Was that a
success?
Maybe.
But those �fteen sales are just the tip of the iceberg. Using Dean’s
research as a guide, there are another 85 potential customers
already on your list who will buy sometime after 90 days.
Think about that for a moment and let the lesson really sink in.
If you knew — KNEW — that the �rst sales were a small fraction
of the sales you’ve already set in motion, would you communicate
to your audience differently?
There’s another inescapable conclusion from Dean’s observation
— we’re far more likely to undermine future sales by trying to
persuade / coerce sales immediately with typical high-pressure
tactics than we are to generate more sales.
You’ve probably heard the expression that you don’t get a second
chance to make a �rst impression. Knowing that, why (WHY?!)
would we ever try to sell to the 15% at the expense of the 85%?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 7 --

That doesn’t make sense…
Now that we understand the problem, what should we do instead?
We’re glad you asked.
The real opportunity is to form a bond, to emphasize our
willingness to create a relationship, to build rapport, and
establish credibility and authority — �rst.
Long before money changes hands.
When you embrace this idea, there’s nothing to lose — people who
are going to buy immediately will buy anyway. Some percentage
of your audience — approximately 1/5 by Dean Jackson’s estimates
— will buy quickly.
In some markets, that percentage may be (signi�cantly) higher,
and in other markets, it may be lower. But, the actual percentage
doesn’t matter — what’s important is that the people who are
going to buy quickly are likely to do that as long as we don’t give
them reasons not to!
The larger opportunity are the others who aren’t going to buy
immediately, no matter what we do. We can increase the
likelihood they’ll buy when they are ready when we treat them
well and give them many opportunities to know, like, and trust us.
We explained this idea, in part, in the Art of Email Manifesto:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 7 --

When we recognize that the overwhelming majority of people who
choose to enter our world are unlikely to become customers quickly,
an entirely new realm of possibility opens.
Instead of rushing to make a sale that’s unlikely to happen (and
almost certainly undermining future sales along the way), we can
focus on creating relationships before transactions.
The seemingly simple decision changes EVERYTHING that follows.
Building relationships with our audience �rst frees up our time and
energy to:
▪ Focus on — and speak to — the needs and desires of our
audience.
▪ Establish genuine empathy, credibility, authority, trust, and
rapport.
▪ Lead with value with no expectation of immediate �nancial
reward.
▪ Treat everyone like a customer regardless if money has changed
hands.
These actions lead to three profound e�ects.
First, it’s a LOT more fun and energizing to engage with audiences
following the Golden Rule — “do unto others as you’d have them do
unto you.”
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 7 --

In the Art of Email we call this principle “people, not metrics,”
recognizing that every name on your list is a real person with real
problems to solve and desires to ful�ll.
People just like you and us.
Second, you’ll turbo-charge your short-term sales (probably a lot). Yes,
that’s counter-intuitive, but something magical happens when you
genuinely treat people exceptionally well. (You may �nd people
reaching out saying “Dudes, Take My Money.”)
Third, when you focus on building and improving relationships (and
other principles taught in the Art of Email), you’ll quickly learn that
it’s much easier (and far more powerful) when transactions emerge
organically vs. trying to engineer sales deliberately.
Trust and attention are earned — there is no shortcut — and
earning both takes time, patience, and a willingness to invest in
the long term betterment of the people we seek to serve.
In practice, the strategy is to adopt a posture of service, not
selling.
When we truly embrace and act on this idea, audience
engagement increases. That engagement is a re�ection of
attention and trust that has been earned.
More and more of our audience will want to move closer to us,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 7 --

our products, and our services.
One last thought before we wrap up this section.
It is critically important that emphasizing relationships before
transactions is genuine. We’re absolutely not trying to ‘game the
system’ by pretending something that’s not true so we can make
more sales.
That’s exactly the wrong way to think about this idea.
Instead, when we genuinely care about an audience, and take the
time to identify what matters to them, magic happens. That magic
must be experienced to be believed.
Please post your comments and questions below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 7 --
